knowledge.statement: Observability is the ability to understand what a system is doing from its external outputs. A system is observable when you can ask new questions about it without adding new instrumentation.

The three pillars of observability are logs (discrete events), metrics (aggregated numeric measurements), and traces (request journeys across services). Together they provide complementary views of system behaviour.
Structured logging emits log entries as machine-readable key-value pairs (JSON). Structured logs can be queried, filtered, and aggregated programmatically, unlike free-form text strings.
Log levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL) should be used consistently. Production systems typically run at INFO; DEBUG output in production floods log storage and obscures real errors.
Logs should capture the 'what happened' but never include sensitive data (PII, passwords, tokens). Log sanitisation must happen before writing, not as a post-processing filter that can fail.
A correlation ID (trace ID) should be attached to every log entry and propagated across service calls. It allows all events from a single user request to be reconstructed across multiple services.
Metrics are numeric measurements collected over time (counter, gauge, histogram, summary). They are cheap to store and fast to query, making them ideal for dashboards and alerting.
The four golden signals for service health are latency, traffic, errors, and saturation (LETS). These four metrics cover most production issues and are the starting point for any service dashboard.
Use percentile latency (p50, p95, p99) rather than average latency for service health signals. Averages mask tail latency; the slowest 1% of requests often represents the worst user experience.

Histograms bucket observations into ranges and allow accurate percentile calculation. Summaries compute percentiles client-side and cannot be aggregated across instances. Prefer histograms for latency metrics in distributed systems.
Prometheus follows a pull model: it scrapes metrics endpoints at intervals. It is the de facto standard for metrics in Kubernetes environments. PromQL is its query language.

Distributed tracing records the path of a request through multiple services as a tree of spans. Each span records operation name, start/end time, and metadata. OpenTelemetry is the standard instrumentation API.

OpenTelemetry (OTel) provides vendor-neutral APIs and SDKs for logs, metrics, and traces. Instrumenting with OTel allows switching backends (Jaeger, Zipkin, Tempo, Datadog) without changing application code.
A span represents a single unit of work within a trace. Key fields: trace_id (ties all spans in a request), span_id, parent_span_id, operation name, start/end timestamps, status, and attributes.

Sampling in distributed tracing collects only a fraction of traces to control storage costs. Head-based sampling decides at the trace root; tail-based sampling decides after the trace is complete, allowing error traces to always be kept.
Service Level Indicators (SLIs) are the specific metrics that measure service quality from the user's perspective (e.g. request success rate, p99 latency). SLIs are the inputs to SLOs.
Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are target values for SLIs over a time window (e.g. 99.9% of requests succeed within 200ms over 30 days). SLOs are internal agreements that drive reliability work.

Service Level Agreements (SLAs) are contractual commitments to customers, typically looser than internal SLOs. If the SLO is 99.9%, the SLA might commit to 99.5% to leave a buffer for negotiation and unforeseen events.

An error budget is the allowable amount of unreliability in an SLO period (e.g. 0.1% of requests may fail). When the error budget is exhausted, reliability work takes priority over new feature development.

Burn rate measures how quickly an error budget is being consumed relative to the SLO window. A burn rate of 1 means the budget will be exhausted exactly at the end of the window; >1 means it will be exhausted early.

Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting (1h/5% burn rate OR 6h/2% burn rate) reduces both false positives and detection latency compared to single-window threshold alerts. It is the Google SRE recommended alerting model.
Alerts should be actionable: every alert should have a runbook, an owner, and a clear action to take. Alerts with no action create alert fatigue and cause real pages to be ignored.

Alert fatigue occurs when too many low-signal alerts train on-call engineers to ignore pages. Reduce alert count by raising thresholds, adding time-based suppression, and deleting alerts that do not require human action.

Symptom-based alerting fires on user-visible symptoms (high error rate, slow latency) rather than causes (CPU usage, disk space). Alert on symptoms; investigate causes. Cause-based alerts produce false positives.

The USE method (Utilisation, Saturation, Errors) is a framework for analysing resource health (CPUs, disks, network interfaces). For every resource, check: how busy it is, how full its queue is, and whether it is erroring.
The RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration) applies to services and endpoints: how many requests per second, how many are failing, and how long they take. RED is the user-facing counterpart to USE.
Dashboards should answer specific operational questions, not display every available metric. A good on-call dashboard shows the four golden signals for each service on a single page.
Grafana is the standard open-source visualisation layer for metrics (Prometheus, InfluxDB), logs (Loki), and traces (Tempo). Grafana dashboards should be stored as code and version controlled.

The ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) and the Grafana Loki stack are the two most common open-source log aggregation solutions. Loki is cheaper at scale because it does not index log content.
Centralized log aggregation collects logs from all services and machines into a single queryable store. Without centralisation, debugging a distributed request requires SSHing into multiple machines.

Application Performance Monitoring (APM) tools (Datadog, New Relic, Dynatrace) combine metrics, traces, and logs in a single product with automatic instrumentation. They reduce setup effort at the cost of vendor lock-in.

Health check endpoints (/health/live and /health/ready) expose application health for orchestrators and load balancers. Liveness reports whether the process is alive; readiness reports whether it can serve traffic.
Synthetic monitoring runs scripted transactions against production endpoints at regular intervals from external locations. It detects outages and latency regressions before real users are impacted.
Real User Monitoring (RUM) collects performance data from actual user browsers (page load time, Core Web Vitals, JS errors). It captures the true user experience, which synthetic tests can miss.

Error tracking tools (Sentry, Bugsnag, Rollbar) capture unhandled exceptions with full stack traces, breadcrumbs, and user context. They complement log aggregation by grouping similar errors and tracking regressions.

Logging context propagation attaches business-relevant identifiers (user_id, tenant_id, request_id) to every log entry in a request. It turns isolated log lines into a coherent story about what a specific user experienced.

Cardinality is the number of unique label combinations in a metric. High-cardinality labels (user_id, request_id) on metrics explode storage and memory usage. Metrics are for aggregates; use traces for high-cardinality data.

Runbooks describe the steps to diagnose and resolve specific alert conditions. They should live next to the alert definition and be kept up to date. A runbook is the difference between a 5-minute and a 45-minute incident.

Post-mortems (incident reviews) analyse outages without assigning blame. The output is a timeline, root cause analysis, contributing factors, and action items. Blameless post-mortems improve reliability without discouraging transparency.

Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and Mean Time to Restore (MTTR) measure incident response effectiveness. Improving MTTD requires better alerting; improving MTTR requires runbooks, on-call training, and system simplicity.
On-call rotations should distribute load fairly and include an escalation path. Engineers should not be on-call for systems they did not build and do not understand. You build it, you run it.
SLO-based alerting replaces ad-hoc threshold alerts. Instead of alerting when CPU > 80%, alert when the error budget burn rate exceeds acceptable levels. This directly aligns alerts with user impact.

Metric naming conventions should be consistent: use snake_case, include the unit in the name (request_duration_seconds, memory_bytes_used), and follow the prometheus naming guide for interoperability.
Span attributes in traces should include the HTTP method, URL, status code, database query type, external service name, and user-facing operation name. Rich attributes are what make traces queryable.

The DORA four metrics (deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, MTTR) are the gold standard for measuring engineering team delivery performance. MTTR specifically measures observability effectiveness.

Feature flag observability means logging flag evaluations and correlating rollout percentages with service health. A new flag should always be accompanied by a dashboard panel showing its activation alongside error rate.

Database observability includes slow query logs, query plans, connection pool saturation, lock contention, and replication lag. Most database outages are preceded by signals in these metrics hours before the failure.

Profiling captures where CPU time and memory allocations are spent in production code. Continuous profiling (Parca, Pyroscope) enables always-on profiling with low overhead for root-causing performance regressions.
Cost observability tracks cloud spend by service, team, and environment. Without it, cost overruns are discovered in monthly bills rather than in real time. Tag resources and set budgetary alerts.
